wpPost.title: Spine Tumor Surgery In Mira Road​
wpPost.content:
Living with persistent back pain or nerve symptoms can be frightening, especially when the cause is unclear. For many people, the words spine tumor bring immediate fear, even before they fully understand what it means. The truth is that spine tumors are complex, but they are also treatable. And with the right specialist, the journey toward diagnosis and recovery becomes much easier to navigate.
In Mira Road and nearby regions, people often delay evaluation because they assume their pain is caused by posture, long hours of sitting, or a minor strain. But when symptoms persist, spread to the limbs, or worsen over time, it may be the body’s way of signaling something deeper happening inside the spine. Early evaluation is not just recommended; it is essential for protecting spinal function and preventing complications.
This blog is meant to help you understand everything you need to know about spine tumors and their treatment. Whether you are researching for yourself or supporting a family member, this information will guide you step by step. The goal is not only to educate you but also to reassure you that modern spine care has advanced tremendously, offering safer and more effective surgical options than ever before.
And at the center of this blog is the experience and expertise of Dr. Manojkumar Gaddikeri, a highly trusted Spine Specialist and Orthopedic Surgeon serving patients in Mira Road and the Mumbai region. His scientific approach, compassionate communication style, and years of specialized training make him one of the most reliable experts for spine tumor diagnosis and surgery.
Let us start by understanding what spine tumors actually are.

What Are Spine Tumors?

A spine tumor is an abnormal growth of cells located in or around the spinal column. These tumors can originate within the spine itself or spread from another part of the body. Some grow slowly and remain confined for years, while others may grow more rapidly and affect nerves or spinal stability.
Spine tumors are classified based on their location. Tumors inside the spinal cord are called intramedullary tumors, while those surrounding the spinal cord but inside the protective layers are called intradural extramedullary tumors. Tumors located outside the spinal cord covering, but still inside the spinal column, are known as extradural tumors. Each behaves differently, which is why accurate diagnosis is the most important first step.
Not all spine tumors are cancerous. Many are benign and treatable with timely intervention. However, even a benign tumor can press on nerves or the spinal cord, causing pain, numbness, weakness, or difficulty walking. This is why early detection plays a crucial role in achieving good outcomes.
Dr. Manojkumar Gaddikeri emphasizes that a tumor inside the spine does not always mean a life-threatening condition. Instead, he explains to patients that understanding the type and behavior of the tumor helps determine the best course of treatment. And when surgery is required, modern techniques can remove or reduce the tumor while protecting normal spinal tissue.

Common Symptoms of Spine Tumors

Symptoms depend on the size, type, and location of the tumor. The earliest signs are often subtle, which is why many people ignore them at first.
One of the most common symptoms is persistent back pain that does not respond to rest or medication. People often describe this pain as deep and constant, sometimes worsening at night. Tumors pressing on nerves may cause tingling, numbness, or weakness, usually in the arms or legs. A feeling of heaviness or difficulty walking may also appear.
Some people notice that their pain worsens when they lie down or try to bend. Others may experience bladder or bowel changes when the tumor affects specific nerve pathways. While these symptoms can also occur due to other spinal conditions, it is important not to ignore them.
Dr. Gaddikeri always reminds patients that pain is the body’s early warning system. When your back pain is accompanied by unexplained neurological symptoms, immediate evaluation can prevent irreversible nerve damage. A spine MRI is usually the most accurate test to detect any abnormal growth.

Why Early Diagnosis Matters

The spine is one of the most delicate and complex structures in the body. Even small tumors can have significant effects if they compress the spinal cord or nerve roots. Early diagnosis gives you time to choose the right treatment, prevents complications, and improves the chances of a full recovery.
When tumors are detected early, surgery becomes more precise and the risk of nerve injury is lower. In cases where the tumor is malignant, early treatment prevents spread and improves survival rates. Early diagnosis also reduces pain and restores mobility faster.
Many patients who meet Dr. Gaddikeri often wish they had come sooner. They usually realize that what they assumed was a simple back strain was actually the beginning of a more serious condition. The good news is that timely medical intervention can prevent long-term disability and return patients to a normal, active lifestyle.

Types of Spine Tumors

Understanding the different types can help you make sense of the diagnosis when speaking with a specialist.
Primary spine tumors originate in the spine itself. They may be benign, such as meningiomas or schwannomas, or malignant, such as chordomas. Secondary tumors, also called metastatic tumors, spread from cancers in the breast, lung, kidney, or other areas. These are more common in adults.
Tumors can also be categorized by location. Intramedullary tumors start inside the spinal cord and tend to be rare. Intradural extramedullary tumors grow near the spinal cord but outside its tissues. Extradural tumors usually involve the vertebrae and can weaken spinal bones, increasing the risk of fractures.
Each type demands a different approach. Some tumors require microsurgery, others require decompression, and some may need stabilization procedures to maintain spinal alignment. Dr. Gaddikeri evaluates each patient individually, ensuring that every treatment plan is tailored to the tumor’s behavior and the patient’s overall health.

How Spine Tumors Are Diagnosed

Diagnosis begins with a detailed conversation about your symptoms, medical history, and any red flags that may point toward a spinal tumor. Dr. Gaddikeri pays close attention to even the smallest signs because early detection can transform a patient’s long-term outcome.
The most important imaging test is MRI, which shows soft tissues, nerves, and the spinal cord in great detail. It can precisely locate the tumor and help determine its type. CT scans may be used to assess the bone structure, especially if the tumor affects vertebrae. Sometimes blood tests or biopsies are required to confirm the diagnosis.
The diagnostic process is not just about identifying the tumor. It is also about understanding how it interacts with the spinal cord and surrounding tissues. This information helps the surgeon plan the safest and most effective surgical approach.

When Surgery Becomes Necessary

Not all tumors require surgery. Small benign tumors that are stable and asymptomatic may only need observation. But when a tumor presses on the spinal cord or nerves, grows rapidly, causes weakness or paralysis, or threatens spinal stability, surgery becomes the right and necessary choice.
Tumor surgery aims to relieve pressure on nerves, remove as much of the tumor as possible, and restore spinal stability. In some cases, a complete tumor removal is possible. In others, partial removal combined with radiation or other treatments may be recommended.
Dr. Gaddikeri explains the surgical plan to patients in simple language, making sure they understand why surgery is necessary and what results to expect. He believes transparency builds trust, especially when patients and families are already dealing with stress and uncertainty.

Advanced Surgical Techniques Used

Modern spine surgery is far more precise and safe than it was even a decade ago. With advancements in imaging, instrumentation, and surgical planning, outcomes have dramatically improved.
Microsurgery is often used for tumors inside or near the spinal cord. It allows the surgeon to work with smaller incisions and finer tools, reducing trauma to healthy tissues. Minimally invasive techniques are used when possible, helping patients recover faster and experience less postoperative discomfort.
In cases where the tumor weakens the spine, stabilization procedures such as instrumentation or fusion may be necessary. These procedures reinforce the spine and prevent future deformity or instability. Dr. Gaddikeri chooses the most conservative yet effective approach depending on the tumor’s characteristics.

What to Expect Before the Surgery

Preparation plays a big role in the smoothness of the surgical experience. Before surgery, the medical team will check your fitness levels, review your medications, and assess your overall health. Imaging studies are reviewed again to plan each step of the surgery with precision.
Patients often feel anxious before surgery, and that is natural. Dr. Gaddikeri spends time addressing all concerns, answering questions, and helping patients feel mentally prepared. Understanding the procedure reduces fear and builds confidence.
You may be advised to avoid certain medications and follow specific guidelines the night before surgery. The care team ensures that everything is clearly explained so that you do not feel uncertain at any stage.
